prompt: The ring of integers O_K of a number field K is:
answer: The integral closure of Z in K (all algebraic integers contained in K).
choices: The integral closure of Z in K (all algebraic integers contained in K).
Just Z itself.
K itself.
The polynomials in a primitive element with Q coeffs.
explanation: Step 1: Definition as integral closure. Step 2: It is the maximal order, Dedekind domain. Final answer: The integral closure of Z in K (all algebraic integers contained in K).

prompt: O_K is always a free Z-module of rank equal to the degree n=[K:Q]. This means there is an integral basis ω1 ... ωn such that every element of O_K is uniquely a1 ω1 +...+ an ωn with ai in Z.
choices: True.
False, it may not be free.
True only for n=2.
The rank is always 1.
explanation: Step 1: Any order in number field (f.g. Z-submodule of K containing 1, stable under mult) is free of rank n by structure theorem or Minkowski. Step 2: O_K is the maximal such. Final answer: True.

prompt: If K=Q(α) with α alg int, then Z[α] is an order (subring) of O_K, but may have finite index >1 in O_K. In that case the integral basis is not simply the power basis 1,α,α^2,...,α^{n-1}.
choices: True.
False, Z[α] is always the full O_K.
True only if α is quadratic.
The index is always infinite.
explanation: Step 1: α integral ⇒ Z[α] ⊆ O_K, and both full rank, index finite. Step 2: Example Q(sqrt5), Z[sqrt5] proper sub of O_K. Final answer: True.

prompt: A rational prime p ramifies in K (i.e. p O_K has a repeated prime ideal factor) if and only if p divides the discriminant of K.
choices: True.
False, ramification is independent of disc.
True only for p odd.
Only unramified primes divide the disc.
explanation: Step 1: The discriminant is the product over ramified primes (with exponents involving e,f and wild ramif). Step 2: Standard theorem: disc =0 mod p iff ramified. Final answer: True.

prompt: To compute an integral basis starting from Z[α], one examines the primes p dividing the index or disc(f), and looks for elements of the form (a0 + a1 α + ... )/p that satisfy a monic equation over Z but are not in the current order. This is done p-adically or via criteria.
choices: True.
False, the power basis is always integral basis.
True but only globally without local methods.
It requires factoring the class number.
explanation: Step 1: The round 2 algorithm or Dedekind criterion checks for each p if the order is p-maximal. Step 2: If not, adjoin the extra elements with denom p that are integral, update the basis and disc. Final answer: True.
